text: CUFFO, a town of Turkey-in-Europe, in the sanj. of Salonika, on the E side of the gulf of Cassandra, in a small bay to the N of Cape Drepano.

CUGAND, a commune of France, in the dep. of Vendee, cant. of Montaigu, on the l. bank of the Sevre-Nantaise, 33 m. NNE of Bourbon-Vendee. Pop. 1,943. It has a paper and a spinning mill, and some manufactories of cloth.

CUGAT-DEL-VALLS (SAN), a town of Spain, in Catalonia, in the prov. and 7 m. NW of Barcelona, partido of Tarrasa. Pop. 2,252. It has a parish-church, a monastery, and a custom-house. It is of Roman origin, and was at one period a place of considerable importance.

CUGES, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of the Bouches-du-Rhone, cant. of Aubagne, 19 m. E of Marseilles. Pop. in 1836, 1,804. The streets of the village are narrow, but it has a fine square and public fountain, and an ancient castle, in a good state of preservation. It has some trade in rosin and capers.
CUGGIONE, a town of Venetian-Lombardy, in the gov., delegation and 20 m. W of Milan, and 11 m. ENE of Novara. Pop. 2,500. It has a manufactory of silk.

CUGLIERI, a town of the island of Sardinia, cap. of the intendenza and prefettura of the same name, 19 m. N of Oristano and 40 m. S of Sassari. It was the Gurulis of Ptolemy. Pop. 2,405.—The surrounding district is extremely fertile, and is noted for its oil.
CUGNAUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Garonne, cant. of Toulouse. Pop. 882. It affords wine in great abundance.
CUGNY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Aisne, cant. of Saint-Simon. Pop. 1,028. It has a tile-work.
CUGUEN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Combourg, 24 m. SE of St. Malo. Pop. 1,474.

CUIABA', or CUYABA, a city of Brazil, the cap. of the prov. of Mato-Grosso, situated in S lat. 15° 36', near the river of the same name. It contains a church, and 3 chapels, all, as well as the houses, built of taipe: the streets are mostly paved. It is the residence of a bishop in partibus, and a juiz-defora. The town and comarca contained, in 1797, about 18,000 persons. Their pop. is at present about 25,000. The orange-tree grows here luxuriantly; the melon, water-melon, and pine-apple also flourish, and mandioc, maize, cotton, and sugar are cultivated. The gold-mines of C., which led the adventurous Paulistas to form settlements in this remote district, were first discovered in 1718, by a certanista, while proceeding up the river C. in search of Indians. The first explorers of the country found vast quantities of gold. In 1723, the prodigious amount of the royal fifths received at San Paulo, excited in that city a universal passion for mining. "Every one was desirous of becoming a miner of C., notwithstanding the calamities attached to so laborious and prolonged a vogage. Of more than 300 persons who, in 1725, departed from San Paulo, in upwards of 20 canoes, only 2 white men and 3 Negroes escaped; all the rest were killed or made prisoners in an encounter with an armada of Payagoa Indians in the river Paraguay." In 1727, the governor of San Paulo, Rodrigo Cesar | 5,995; in 1841, 5,883. 770 CUL de Menzes, arrived at the new arraial, to which he gave the name of Villa Real de C. In 1730, a party of Paulistas, with the late ouvidor of the town at their head, returning from C. with upwards of 60 arrobas, or nearly 2,000 lbs. weight of gold, were attacked, near the mouth of the Tacoari, by an armada of 80 war-canoes, manned by more than 800 Indians; and only 17 whites escaped by swimming to land. The Payogoas disposed of the gold in the city of Assumption at so low a rate, that 6 lbs. weight of the precious metal was exchanged by one Indian for a pewter plate. In 1732, another party of Paulistas, consisting of 50 canoes, was cut off by the Indians: a few only escaping to C. It was now deemed necessary to send a powerful armament to clear the Paraguay of these formidable marauders. A desperate conflict took place in 1735, terminating, however, to the advantage of the Paulistas. In the same year, a road was opened to the territory of Goyaz; and intelligence being brought of new gold-mines being discovered in Mato-Grosso, almost the whole pop. of C. left that town for the W.—The river C. rises in the district of Diamantino, nearly in the same lat. as the Paraguay, and flows SSW to join the Rio Porrudos, or San Lorenzo, on the r. bank in S lat. 17° 20'. Its tributaries are the Cuiaba-Mirim, the Tutez, Coxipo, Guaxu, and Caranda.
CUIATE', a parish and village of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, on the l. bank of a river of the same name, and 12 m. above its junction with the Rio-Doce. Pop. 2,000.
CUICHUX, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Pyrénées-Orientales, cant. of La Tour-de-France, and com. of Cassauges. Pop. with that com. 349. It has some mineral springs.
CUILLE', a commune of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, cant. of Cossé-le-Vivien, 25 m. NW of Chateau-Gontier. Pop. 1,586.

CUISANCIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Doubs, cant. of Beaume-les-Dames, situated at the source of the Cuisancin, in a fine valley, in which are the sulphureous baths of Guillon. Pop. 123. It contains the ruins of a Benedictine abbey, and has an extensive paper-mill.
CUISE-LA-MOTTE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Oise, cant. of Attichy. Pop. 1,052.
CUISEAUX, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire, arrond. of Louhans. The cant. comprises 9 com. Pop. in 1841, 10,663. The com. is 12 m. SE of Louhans. Pop. 1,753.

CUISERY, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire, arrond. of Louhans. The cant. comprises 10 com. Pop. in 1841, 9,721. The town is 9 m. SW of Louhans, on the r. bank of the Seille. Pop. 1,732.

CUKENAM, a river of Guayana, which rises in a mountain of the same name, and flowing SW forms with the Yuruani the river Caroni, a tributary of the Orinoco. Its banks, even where 20 ft. high, are overflown during the wet season.
CULAN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cher, cant. of Chateau-Meillant. Pop. 1,169.

CULBORNE, or KILNER, a parish in Somersetshire, 9 m. W of Minehead, on Porlock bay, near the Bristol channel. Area 1,560 acres. Pop. 34. This place, celebrated for its romantic scenery, is surrounded by perpendicular cliffs from 1,200 to 1,300 ft. in height.
CULCHETH, a township in the p. of Winwick, Lancashire, 5 m. W of Newton, close on the Liverpool and Manchester railway. Area 5,330 acres. Pop. 2,193.
CULDAFF, a parish and v. in co. Donegal, 8 m. NNW of Moville. Area 2,089 acres. Pop. in 1831,
CUL-DE-SAC, a name given to the plain which extends between the town of Port-au-Prince in the island of Hayti, and the mountains to the NE.

CUL-DE-SAC (GRAND), a bay on the N of the isthmus which unites the E and W portions of the island of Guadalupe. It communicates by a narrow channel with the Petit C., on the opposite side of the isthmus.
CUL-DE-SAC-A-VACHE, a bay on the coast of Martinique, in N lat. 14° 31', W long. 62° 5'.
CUL-DE-SAC-DES-ANGLOIS, a bay on the SE coast of Martinique, between Cape Ferré and the Pointe-des-Salines.
CUL-DE-SAC-FRANCAIS (LE), a town of Martinique, in the quarter and 15 m. SE of La Trinité, on a small gulf of the same name.
CUL-DES-SARTS (LE), a commune and village of Belgium, in the dep. of Namur, arrond. and 18 m. S of Philippeville. Pop. 889.

CULEBRA, a port of Costa-Rico, on the Pacific ocean, at the mouth of a small stream of the same name, in N lat. 10° 35', W long. 85° 38'. It is 1\frac{1}{2} league wide at its mouth, and is divided into 3 channels by some islands. The entrances are clear and deep, and the interior, in which 200 ships may ride in from 10 to 12 fath. water, is sheltered from all winds.—Also an islet, 6 furlongs NNW of Culebra point, in N lat. 9° 34′ 30″, W long. 79° 17′.

CULEBRA (SIERRA-DE-LA), a mountain-range of Spain, which stretches from the NW part of the prov. of Zamora to the S of the Tera river, and gives origin to several considerable streams, among which are the Aliste, an affluent of the Esla, and the Manzanas.

CULEBRA, or SERPENT'S ISLAND, an island off the E coast of Porto-Rico, in the Virgin's passage. It is 6 m. in length, and has numerous kays and reefs about it.—Off its E side is an islet called Culebrita.
CULEBRAS (POINT), the E extremity of St. Louis island, in the bay of Galveston, on the coast of Texas.
CULFEIGHTRIN, a parish in co. Antrim, 13 m. E of Ballycastle. Area 26,337 acres. Pop. in 1831, 5,012; in 1841, 4,577.

CULFORD, a parish and v. in the co. of Suffolk, 5 m. NNW of Bury-St.-Edmund's. Area of p. 2,490 acres. Pop. 352. The v. of C. is remarkably neat, being recently built on a uniform plan, each house containing 4 rooms.
CULGAITH, a chapelry in the p. of Kirkland, co. of Cumberland, 61 m. E by S of Penrith, bounded W by the Eden, and S by the Tees. Pop. 404.
CULGRUFF CREEK, an islet on the coast of Arctic America, in N lat. 66° 51′ 06", W long. 84° 43' 19". It is 6 m. in length, and 3 m. in breadth.
CULGRUFF POINT, a headland on the coast of Arctic America, in N lat. 69° 46′ 19", W long. 98° 32' 49".
CULHAM, a parish in Oxfordshire, 14 m. SSE of Abingdon, bounded S by the Thames. Area 1,680 acres. Pop. 396.
CULHAT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Lezoux. Pop. 1,362.

CULIACAN, a town of Mexico, in the state of Sonora-et-Sinaloa, in N lat. 24° 56', on a rising ground on the l. bank of a river of the same name, a little below the confluence of the Rio-Mayo. Pop. about 5,000, according to Gillian. It was the cap. of Sinaloa while a separate dep.—The C. river rises to the E of Durango; and flows, W by N in a wide deep bed, to the town of C., when it turns to the W of S., and flows into the gulf of California, 100 m. below C., in N lat. 24° 30'. It has a course of about 250 m.
CULLA, a town of Spain, in Valencia, in the prov. and 30 m. NW of Castellon-de-la-Plana, near the r. bank of the Monleon. Pop. 900. アプラグラグ リティスス アントン カンタン アントララント アンコンピンピンピーピーピーピーピーピーピー CUL
CULLAR-DE-BAZA, a village of Spain, in Grenada, in the prov. and 54 m. N of Almeria.
CULLATUR, or MUDUCULLATUR, a town of Hindostan, in the Carnatic, in the district of Tinnevelly, 25 m. W of Ramnad.

CULLEN, a parish and burgh in Banffshire, on the coast. Pop. of p. in 1801, 1,076; in 1841, 1,564. -The burgh is 14 m. W by N of Banff, on the postroad from Fochabers to Banff. It unites with Elgin, Banff, Kintore, Peterhead, Macduff, and Inverury, in returning a member. Electors in 1847, 38.

CULLEN, a parish in co. Cork, 3 m. N of Millstreet. Area 13,674 acres. Pop. in 1831, 4,385; in 1841, 5,490.—Also a p. in co. Cork, 5 m. NNE of Kinsale. Area 4,250 acres. Pop. in 1831, 1,251; in 1841, 1,330.—Also a p. in co. Tipperary, 3 m. NW of Tipperary. Area 1,986 acres. Pop. in 1831, 1,412; in 1841, 1,013.
CULLEN'S-WOOD, a village in the p. of St. Peter, co. Dublin, 1 m. SSE of Dublin. Pop. 546.
CULLENWAINE, a parish, partly in co. Tipperary, and partly in co. Leinster. Area 4,745 acres. Pop. 2,327.
CULLERA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 24 m. SSE of Valencia, at the embouchure of the Jucar. Pop. 9,400.
CULLERA (CAPE), a headland of Spain, on the coast of Valencia, 3 m. N of the town of C., in N lat. 39° 9.'
CULLERCOATS, a township in the p. of Tynemouth, co. of Northumberland, 11 m. NNW of Tynemouth. Pop. 730.
CULLEYBACKEY, a village in the p. of Ahoghill, co. Antrim. Pop. 238.
CULLINSTOWN, a parish in co. Meath, 42 m. S by W of Duleek. Area 1,200 acres. Pop. in 1831, 298; in 1841, 366.

CULLODEN, a large moory ridge in the p. of Croy, from 3 to 5 m. E of Inverness, memorable for the total defeat of Prince Charles's army, on the 16th April, 1746, by the King's troops under the Duke of Cumberland. It is sometimes called Drummossie moor. The spot selected by Charles for the engagement was about 1 m. to the S of Culloden house, on a ridge of the moor declining towards the river Nairn on the S. Immediately to the S of his position was a square enclosure of stone which extended to the banks of the Nairn, and the N wall of which covered his right flank In his front the moor was marshy and soft; and on the left, though at a considerable distance, were the woods of Culloden-house. The battle was fought just where the general surface of the moor begins to incline towards the river Nairn. A new carriage - road from Inverness has recently been made through it, which touches the principal line of graves at their N extremity. On all sides the prospect is bleak and dreary; while the general smoothness of the ground points it out as favourable for the movements of cavalry and artillery, but proportionably ill-adapted for the protection or defence of the foot-soldier. Such is the nature of the ground on which Prince Charles Edward ventured to peril his cause against the disciplined troops of England. His army was drawn up a little to the W of the graves, in a line across the moor inclining towards the parks of Culloden-house.
CULLODEN, a village in Monroe co., in the state of Georgia, U. S., 68 m. W of Milledgeville.-Also a v. in Bradley co., in Tennessee, 150 m. SE of Nashville.
CULLOHILL, a village in the p. of Aughmacart, Queen's co. Pop. in 1831, 130.
CULLY, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, 6 m. SW of Lausanne. Pop. 896.
CULLYHANNA, a village in the p. of Creggan, co. Armagh. Pop. 154.

CULM, or Kulm, a town and circle of Prussia, in the gov. of Marienwerder, prov. of W Prussia. The area of the circle is about 341 sq. m. Pop. 33,200. It is watered by the Vistula on the W, where the soil is rich and productive. Grain, flax, tobacco, and fruit are raised, and cattle are extensively reared. -The town of C. stands on a hill at the foot of which the Vistula flows, in N lat. 53° 21'. It was founded by knights of the Teutonic order in 1229. It has 5 Roman Catholic churches, 1 Protestant church, a chapel, 3 nunneries, an infirmary, a Catholic ecclesiastical seminary, a cadet academy founded in 1775 by Frederic II., and 3 other schools. It gives name to the Catholic bishopric of C., the diocesan of which has his residence and chapter at Culmsea. The pop. in 1837 was 5,394. The chief occupation of the pop. are the manufactures of woollens, tanning, fishing, and trading. C. was formerly one of the Hanse towns.

CULMBACH, a bailiwick and town in the N part of the Bavarian circle of Upper-Franconia. The town is situated in a fertile country on the l. bank of the White Main, in N lat. 50° 5'. It is well built. Pop. 4,000, of whom 180 are Roman Catholics. It has 3 churches, a Latin school, an hospital, infirmary, several breweries, potteries, and an earthenware manufactory. In the neighbourhood potters'-clay is largely raised. On a mass of rock close to the town stands an old castle of the Plassenburg, now used as a house-of-correction, in which the prisoners are employed in weaving carpets, coarse woollens, and spinning yarn. The outworks of the castle were razed by the French in 1808.
CULME, a river in Devonshire, which rises in the Black Down hills, and after a course of 251 m. enters the river Exe, near Stoke-Cannon.
CULMINGTON, a parish in Shropshire, 45 m. NNW of Ludlow, on the river Cowe. Area 3,160 acres. Pop. 541.
CULMSEE, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of Prussia, circle of Thorn. Pop. 1,372.
CULMSTOCK, a parish in Devonshire, 7 m. NE of Collumpton, on the river Culme. Area 4,530 acres. Pop. 1,446.
CULOZ, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ain, cant. of Seyssel. Pop. 1,301.

CULPA, or KULPA, a river of Croatia, an affluent of the Save, which it joins on the r. bank, 6 m. ENE of Petrinia. It flows past Freithurn, Motling, and Carlstadt; and receives on the r. the Korana; on the 1. the Lachina, Commicza, Kuspina, and Odra.

CULPEPPER, a county in the NE part of the state of Virginia, U. S., skirted on the NE by the Rappahannock river, and on the S by the Rapid Anne. Area 672 sq. m. The cap. is Fairfax. Pop. 11,393. The chief productions are grain and tobacco. A gold mine recently opened on the Rapid Anne, 17 m. from Fredericksburg, has in the first 7 weeks of its working produced 3,400 dwts. of gold, worth about 3,300 dollars.
CULPHO, a parish in the co. of Suffolk, 4 m. E of Woodbridge. Area 1,620 acres. Pop. 70.

CULROSS, a parish and burgh in Perthshire. The p., bounded on the S by the frith of Forth, has an area of 8,145 Scots acres. Pop. in 1801, 1,502; in 1841, 1,444, of whom 603 were in the burgh of C., 85 in the v. of Blairburn, and 260 in the v. of Lowvalleyfield.—The burgh of C. is 6 m. W of Dunfermline. It is chiefly inhabited by linen and cotton weavers. It unites with Dunfermline, Inverkeithing, South Queensferry, and Stirling, in returning a member to parliament. Electors in 1847, 25. Remains of an abbey founded in 1217 still exist here. CUM
CULSAMOND, a parish in Aberdeenshire, at the source of the Urie. Pop. in 1801, 730; in 1841, 1,104.

CULTER, a parish partly in Lanarkshire, partly in Peebles-shire, intersected by the Culter, a headstream of the Clyde, and rising on the S into high mountains, the loftiest of which, the Fell or Culterfell, has an alt. of 2,430 ft. Pop. in 1801, 369; in 1841, 536, of whom 197 were in the v. of C.—Also a stream in Aberdeenshire, which takes its rise in a loch in the p. of Skene, and falls into the Dee, 6 m. above Aberdeen.
CULTS, a parish in Fifeshire, skirted by the Eden on the NW. Area 2,100 Scots acres. Pop. in 1801, 699; in 1841, 889, of whom 490 were in the v. of Pitlessie.
CULVER (Point), a promontory on the S coast of Australia, in S lat. 32° 57', E long. 124° 39'.
CULVERLANDS-AND-TELFORD, a tything in the p. of Farnham, Surrey, 3 m. ESE of Farnham. Pop. 509.
CULVERTHORPE, a township and chapelry in the p. of Haydon, Lincolnshire, 5 m. SW of Sleaford. Area 860 acres. Pop. 139.
CULWORTH, a parish in Northamptonshire, 73 m. NE of Banbury. Area 2,060 acres. Pop. 713.

CUMA, a fortress in the kingdom and prov. of Naples, about 3\frac{1}{2} m. NW of Pozzuoli, near the coast of the Mediterranean. It occupies the site of the ancient and celebrated city of Cumæ, the first Greek colony in Italy founded by the Chalcidians, and once a place of great commercial wealth and importance owing to its maritime situation. Under the Romans it declined; and it was ultimately sacked by the Saracens. In the 13th cent. it became the head-quarters of banditti, and the neighbouring cities found it necessary to unite in completing its destruction. The ancient town was seated on a steep and rocky hill, on the summit of which stood the temple of Apollo. The plains are strewed with interesting fragments of former grandeur, and crumbling masses of stone overgrown by vegetation. One of the streets of the ancient C. may still be distinctly traced.

CUMANA', a province of Venezuela, comprising the NE portion of that republic; and bounded on the N by the Caribbean sea; on the E by the Atlantic; on the W by Barcelona; and on the S by the Orinoco and its deltoid branch known as the Boca- Vagre. It extends between 8° 9' and 10° 44' N lat.; and from 2° 54' to 5° 12' E long. of Caracas. Barcelona is included by some writers in the territories of this extensive province, in which case the W boundary is Caracas proper. The great extent of C. and Barcelona-forming what has been called Nueva Andalusia—their being washed on two sides by the ocean, and by the Orinoco on the third, render them a most important portion of the republic. Besides the Orinoco, the coast of C. and Barcelona is pierced by numerous rivers, of which the Unara, separating Caracas from Barcelona, is navigable from San-Antonio-de-Clarimas, 18 m. in the interior. It receives the Ipire at about 45 m. from its source in the mountains, and runs about the same distance thence to the sea. The city of C. stands on the small river Manzanares. The next stream of any importance is the Guarapiche, which flows into the Atlantic by a very broad estuary just above the first mouth of the Orinoco. The Mamo, the Pao, and the Suara, are the largest rivers of this country which flow into the Orinoco. The chief productions of the prov. of C. are sugar, maize, and corn. Its singular peninsular point of Araya, in N lat. 10° 36', and W long. 64° 20', has produced immense quantities of salt ever since its discovery. This spot used to be clandestinely visited by the colonists of every nation which possessed establishments in the W Indies; nor was CONTRACTOR OF THE CONTRACTOR OF THE PROPERTURATION OF THE PROPERTURATION OF THE PROPERTURATION OF THE PROPERTURAL PROPERTURATION OF THE PROPERTURAL PROPERTURAL PROPERTURAL PROPERTURAL PROPERTURAL PROPERTURAL PROPERTURAL PR it until the expulsion of the Dutch in 1605 that the government of C. took absolute possession of it. The great mere or lake of which the salt-works consisted was, however, overflowed in the dreadful hurricane of 1726, which converted it into a gulf several miles long; but vasets or pits and reservoirs have since been dug, so that vast quantities of salt are still pro cured here. The consumption of this article in forming salted provisions amounts in Barcelona and C. to 9,000 or 10,000 fanegas [each of 400 lbs.] annually, of which the salt-grounds of Araya furnish 3.000 fanegas, and the sea the remainder. Araya is also noted as being the first place in which the Spaniards began to found a town; and it contains springs and masses of petroleum, this substance existing also on its coasts at Cape de la Brea, Punta Soto, and Guararitto. A stream of naphtha issues from the bed of the sea near these shores, and forms a visible spot, 100 ft. in diam., among the weeds with which the beach is covered.—Hall estimated the united pop. of Barcelona and C. at 100,000; Dupons at 80,000. In 1841, the pop. of the prov. of C. was 50,671.—The prov. is subdivided into the cantons of C., Cumanacoa, Cariaco, Carupano, Rio - Caribe, Guiria, Aragua, Maturin, and Los Canos.

CUMANA, or NEW TOLEDO, the chief city of the above prov., is situated in N lat. 10° 27' 52", and W long. 64° 9′ 47" [Humboldt]; 1 m. from the embarcadero or mouth of its harbour, between which and the dostan, in the presidency of Madras, prov. and 42 m. town is the great plain of El-Salado, -a salt marsh consisting of whitish sand partially covered with low shrubs. To the E is another extensive plain; and on the N is a rocky mass, on which the castle of San Antonio is built. The city occupies the space between this citadel, the river Manzanares, and another stream called Santa-Catalina. C. is one of the oldest cities on the continent, having been founded by Gonzalo Ocampo in 1520. It contains no very remarkable buildings, but has 2 parish-churches, 2 convents, and a theatre. All the houses are low and slightly built for personal security during the occurrence of earthquakes. It is noted for the purity and healthiness of its climate: the great heat incident to its situation being moderated by the sea breezes, so that the therm. ranges between 70° and 93°; the mean temp. being 81° 30′. A large suburb on the S side of the city, and divided from it by the Manzanares river, is called the town of the Guayguericas, being inhabited by a tribe of Indians of that name, who cultivate the adjoining fertile plains, and whose neat cottages and gardens filled with sapotes, mamies, and plantains, give a beautiful appearance to C. Humboldt observes that the entrance of the harbour of C. is highly picturesque; the city rising out of a plain backed by the citadel, and covered with plantations of cocoa-nuts, cassias, capers, palm-trees, and arborescent mimosas; and the shores covered with alcatras, egrets, and flamingoes. The port is formed by the gulf of Cariaco, which has good anchorage, and is capable of receiving all the navies of Europe. See Cariaco. It is usually smooth and unruffled: the principal danger is a shoal called Morro-Roxo, which is 900 fath. broad from E to W.—C. has suffered dreadfully from earthquakes. The natives have a tradition that the gulf of Cariaco was formed by an earthquake just before the visit of Columbus. The shocks on record which have done the greatest injury to C. were those experienced in 1530, and frequently towards the end of the 16th cent. On the 24th of October 1766, the city was totally overthrown, many persons perished, and the shocks continued hourly for 14 months, till 1767, when they happened only once a-month. In 1794 another tremendous visitation occurred; and on the 14th of December 1797, four-fifths of C. were utterly CUM destroyed. The pop. of C. was estimated by Dupons at 24,000 in 1802; and by Lavaysse in 1810 at 30,000. It is reputed to be at present about 8,000, consisting principally of white Creoles, who are occupied in commerce, navigation, and agriculture. The fisheries give subsistence to a great portion; and large shipments of salted provisions, tassajo or smoked meat, and salted fish, are exported to Caracas and the Windward islands. From the cocoa-nut a considerable quantity of oil is made; and excellent cables are formed of the bark of the mahet or bombax.

CUMANACOA, a town of Venezuela, in the dep. and 22 m. SW of Cumana. Pop. 2,800. It is situated in a naked circular plain, surrounded by lofty mountains, and elevated 104 toises, or 665 ft., above sea-level. Humboldt is of opinion that the valley of C. is the bottom of an ancient lake. The road to it from Cumana lies over the naked ridge called El- Imposible, which may be considered as the key of the llanos of the prov. of Cumana. The most valuable production of the surrounding country is tobacco. Next to it is indigo, which often nearly equals that of Guatimala.
CUMANIA. See KUMANIEN.
CUMBA, a town of Assuay, in the prov. of Jaen, 30 m. SSW of Jaen-de-Bracamoros, on the r. bank of the Maranon.
CUMBALUM, or CULLUMBAUM, a fortress of Hin- SE of Coimbatore, and 21 m. SSW of Daraporam, on the l. bank of the Ambraddy.
CUMBEEBER, or KUNBEIBAH, a village on the N coast of the island of Dhalai in the Red sea.
CUMBER (Lower), a parish in co. Londonderry, about 6 m. SE by E of Londonderry. Area 14,782 acres. Pop. in 1831, 4,584; in 1841, 4,510.
CUMBER (UPPER), a parish partly in co. Tyrone, and partly in co. Londonderry. Area 26,329 acres. Pop. in 1831, 7,745; in 1841, 7,052.

CUMBERLAND, a maritime county at the NW extremity of England. On the N and NW it is bounded by Scotland, from which it is separated by the Solway frith, the Sark, the Eske, the Liddle, and the Kershope-burn. On the E and NE it is bounded by Northumberland, and a small part of Durham; on the SE, by Westmoreland and Lancaster, from the former of which it is separated by the Tees, the Eden, the Eamont, and the Ulleswater, and from the latter by the Duddon. On the W it is bounded by the Irish sea. It lies between 54° 6' and 55° 7' N lat., and 2° 13' and 3° 30' W long. Its extreme length, from the Kershope-burn on the NE to Millom point on the SW, is about 72 m. Its extreme breadth, from Allonby on the Solway, to Nent-head on the borders of Northumberland, is about 38 or 40 m.; and its circumf. about 225 m. It contains 1,523 sq. m., or 974,720 statute acres, two-thirds of which, including however upwards of 340,000 acres of mountain district, are considered to be enclosed and cultivated. Of the remaining third part, including 8,000 acres of lakes, rivers, and moors, the greater part, though it may be considerably improved for pasturage, will in all probability for ever defy the powers of cultivation. Surface, &c.] The surface of this co. is considerably diversified, but in its general aspect, and especially in the E division, it is bleak and sterile, and the climate dry and cold, yet bracing and conducive to health and longevity, as is shown by the report of the registrar-general. The mountains are numerous, and many of them immense in elevation, and singular in structure. They enter into the composition of almost every view; and either by their romantic form, or the imposing, wild, and awful majesty of their appearance, are peculiarly calculated to excite interest and admiration. Two distinct ranges intersect the co. Of these one runs towards the N, to which belongs the Crossfell ridge; the other, and the most gigantic, toward the S, of which the highest peak, Skiddaw, is 3,166 ft. above the level of the sea. Besides this noted peak, the most striking of the loftier mountains—which are here termed fells, and are familiar to tourists and to admirers of picturesque and majestic scenery in general,—are the Seafell, Saddleback, Helvellyn, Bowfell, Grasmerefell and Hardknot, Wrynose, Highpike, Pillar, Screes, &c. The scenery of the C. fells is in general abrupt, precipitous and bold. Between the grander heights are many hills of various elevation; some of them cut through by clear and rapid torrents; others based in richly wooded, highly cultivated valleys, fertilized by quiet brooks and rivers; others dipping their precipitous and craggy sides down into beautiful lakes; the picturesque and varied scenery of which has often been described in poetry and prose. Rivers.] The principal rivers of C. are the Eden, the Scottish Esk, and the Derwent. The Eden, one of the largest rivers in the N of England, rises in Westmoreland, enters Cumberland a little to the S of Edenhall, and flowing by Kirk-Oswald, Warwickbridge, and Carlisle, falls into the Solway frith below Rocliffe. The Esk from Scotland enters C. at a place called the Moat, and, flowing past Longtown, falls into the Solway frith. The Derwent rises in Borrowdale, flows through and forms the lakes of Derwent and Bassenthwaite, and, turning to the SW, meets the Cocker at Cockermouth, whence it flows, by Workington, to the sea. Among the smaller streams are the Petterell, and the Caldew, which join the Eden, the former to the NE, and the latter to the NW of Carlisle; the Croglin, which also joins the Eden to the N of Kirk-Oswald; the Duddon, which skirts the SE border of the co., and forms an estuary at Millom point between this co. and Lancaster; the English Esk, which has its sources on the S of the Seafell, and falls into the Irish sea at Ravenglass; the Jet, famous for its pearl-muscles, which has its source in West-water lake, in Wasdale, to the S of Buttermere, and falls into the sea nearly at the same place; the Ehen, which has its source in the mountains surrounding Borrowdale, and forms Ennerdale-water, to the W of Buttermere, and the Calder, both of which fall into the Irish sea to the N of Ravenglass. Among the mountains are a number of splendid waterfalls, particularly Stock-Gill-force, Rydal waterfalls, and Lowdore cascade, near the SE corner of Derwent-water, which has a fall of 200 ft., and, in a rainy season, is one of the most magnificent objects in Great Britain. The Lakes.] Of these,—for sketches of which see the respective articles,—the principal are Ulleswater, between Cumberland and Westmoreland; Thirlemere, or Leath-water, at the entrance to the vale of St. John; Derwent-water, and Bassenthwaite, connected by a stream in Keswick vale; Borrowdale, in the vale of that name; Buttermere, in the NW of Borrowdale,—flanked by rugged mountains; and to the N, Crummock-water and Lowes-water. The three latter are connected by the Cocker, which, running N through the fine vale of Lorton, falls into the Derwent at Cockermouth. Besides these and Overwater, Wast-water, Osmerdale, and Devock-water, there are many smaller lakes, called tarns. Wild fowl, salmon, trout, and char, are taken in the lakes. The last is a delicate fish, found nowhere, it is said, but in the lakes of Cumberland and Westmoreland: considerable quantities of it are sent from Keswick, through Carlisle, to London. Soil and agriculture.] The soil, like the surface of C., is various. In particular spots it is a strong rich | loam, producing all sorts of grain with very moderate care and preparation. In other places it is a heavy wet loam, upon a cold clay bottom. In the lower districts, and sometimes far up the W mountains, the prevailing soil is light dry loam. In the mountainous districts, particularly on the borders of Durham and Northumberland, the predominating soil is a black peat earth, unmanageable and almost unimprovable.-Moor game is plentiful; and cranberries are so abundant that they are exported to London and elsewhere. Corn is principally grown in the NW parts of the county; but in the NE, turnips are extensively and scientifically cultivated as food for sheep and cattle. The rotation of crops, on good soil, is frequently 3 years' grass, and afterwards, oats, turnips or potatoes, wheat, hay and grass, in succession. Farms in this co. are extremely various in size; but the proportion of those of 500 acres or upwards is very small, though in the lake district a farmer may have moorland pasture almost ad lib., to add to his tilled land. There is a class of landowners in C., who, if not absolutely peculiar to it, far exceed in number and relative importance the ordinary proportion, viz., the small proprietors or yeomanry, who are here known by the appellation of 'statesmen,' that is, men of estate. Most of these occupy estates worth from £10 to £50 a-year, either freehold, or held of the lord-of-the-manor by customary tenure. It is said that this class has been decreasing in numbers during the last 70 years; they are still estimated, however, at nearly 7,000. There is also a large class of small farmers not proprietors, whose holdings are from 40 to 100 acres. The modern farm-houses are of stone, covered with fine blue slates from Borrowdale. A large portion of the co., especially in the SSE and S divisions, is appropriated to grazing; and excellent butter forms a principal item in the exports. The cattle are chiefly the old Cumberland steers, a small breed with long horns. The Galloway and short-horned cattle are prevalent. The sheep, besides the Cheviots, are a black-faced, mixed, and hardy breed, with short coarse wool. Minerals, manufactures, &c.] Almost all the moors and mountains of this co. teem with valuable minerals. A celebrated plumbago or black lead mine is situated at the head of Borrowdale, 9 m. S by W of Keswick, in the steep side of Seatollen-fell, extremely difficult of access. This peculiar substance—which contains no lead whatever, but is composed chiefly of carbon, impregnated with iron, and is hence erroneously called plumbago or black lead, &c., though it is less objectionably also termed graphite—is so rare, as to be almost exclusively procured from this mine; and fears have, groundlessly perhaps, been entertained of its complete exhaustion. The mines are defended from pilferers by temporary mason-work, and a wall within, with a house over each entrance, occupied by the stewards and workmen. Coal, gypsum, limestone, and slate, are all wrought within the co. The export of coal in 1846 from its three ports was 321,835 tons, besides 4,822 tons of culm. The geological characteristics of the district are various; but consist chiefly in its red marl and gypsum, the latter of which is extensively exported from Carlisle. Marble, porcelain clay, quartz, spar, and many other mineral substances are found; with cobalt, antimony, iron, silver, manganese, and copper. Lead, iron, and coal, with limestone, are the staple minerals. The principal lead-mines are those of Alston-moor, which originally belonged to the estate of Derwent-water; but upon the attainder of the earl in 1715, were vested in Greenwich hospital. Many of these mines are worked to great advantage, and they produce annually 6,700 tons. The total number of hands employed in mining in this co. in 1841 was 3,640. Most extensive coal-works are carried on in the neighbourhood | other families 12,164; in 1841, 178,038, of whom of Whitehaven and Workington. In the E quarter of the co. also, there is abundance of coal. In the p. of Egremont is the most singular mine of iron ore in Britain. The thickness of the band of ore, which is hard, solid, and comparatively pure metal, is between 24 and 25 ft.—There are iron-foundries at Carlisle, Dalston, and Seaton. Earthenware articles are manufactured at Dearham and Whitehaven; and bottles at the Ginns. The woollen manufacture is carried on at Alston, Brigham, Caldbeck, Crossthwaite, and Hayton. There are flax manufactories at St. Bees, Brigham, Bridekirk, Cleator, Dalston, and Egremont, and the cotton manufacture has also been established in Carlisle, Dalston, and Wetherall, and employed 3,836 hands in 1841. Canals, roads, and railways.] There is only one canal in this co., namely, that which renders Carlisle an inland port,-the Ship-canal, which runs from Carlisle, past Burgh, Drumburgh, and Kirkland, towards Bowness, and communicates with the Solway frith. The co. is crossed from S to N, and from E to W, by two important turnpike roads, one of which is a principal road from London to Edinburgh and Glasgow, entering near Penrith, and proceeding direct to Carlisle, whence it runs direct to Springfield, near Gretna, where it quits England and enters Scotland. The other road, from Newcastle and the German ocean on the E, enters C. near Nether Denton, passes through Brampton, and crosses the great northern road at Carlisle. It then proceeds by two branches, the one through old Carlisle, joined by the Wigton road, and the other past the Bassenthwaite water to Cockermouth, and thence to Whitehaven and the Irish sea. It is intersected by the Newcastle and Maryport railway through Carlisle; and by the Caledonian railway, a continuation and outlet to Scotland of the Grand Junction line from Lancashire. Divisions, &c.] This co. is divided into an E and a W parliamentary section, each of which sends 2 members to parliament. The polling-places for the former are Carlisle, Wigton, Penrith, Alston, and Brampton, the principal place of election being Carlisle; for the latter, Cockermouth, Keswick, Aspatria, Egremont, and Bootle, the principal place of election being Cockermouth. The number of electors registered for the co. in 1837 was 9,075; in 1842-3, 9,223; in 1846, for E division, 5,348; for W division, 4,042. It is divided into 5 wards, synonymous with hundreds in other counties; but here termed wards, from the inhabitants of each division having been formerly obliged to keep watch or ward against northern irruptions. Of these, the E parliamentary division comprehends the wards of Cumberland, Eskdale, and Leath; the W, Allerdale above, and Allerdale below, Derwent. All the manufacturing and trading part of the co. lies within the 2 latter wards; the remaining 3 are almost purely agricultural. Pop. in Pop. in Area in Wards. With the exception of Allerdale ward above Derwent, which is in the deanery of Copeland, and dio. of Chester, the co. is in the archdeaconry and dio. of Carlisle, and prov. of York. It contains 3 deaneries, Carlisle, Penrith, and Wigton; 41 rectories; 28 vicarages; 59 perpetual curacies and chapelries; 104 parishes; and 125 dissenting and Roman Catholic places of worship. It comprises 1 city and county town, Carlisle; 2 boroughs, Cockermouth and Whitehaven; 14 market-towns; 5 principal ports, Carlisle, an inland port, Whitehaven, Maryport, Workington, and Harrington; and 400 townships. The pop. in 1801 was 117,230; in 1821, 156,124; in 1831, 171,700, of whom 10,630 families were chiefly employed in agriculture, and 12,026 in trade, manufactures, &c., all 153,770 were natives of the co.; 6,355 were Scotch; and 4,881 Irish; 26,053 were returned as engaged in trade, commerce, and manufactures, and 15,611 in agriculture. The annual value of assessed property in 1815 was £705,446; in 1842-3, £910,333. In 1834-8, we find, from an elaborate statistical table of crime composed by Mr. Rawson, that, taking the proportion of all the criminals to the total pop. as unity, or 1.00, C. ranks so low in crime as .49, while the average of 11 agricultural counties stands as high as 1.07. History and Antiquities.] Before Britain was conquered by the Romans, C. was probably occupied by the Caledonians; and Richard of Cirencester states that it formed a part of the territories of a British tribe called the Sistuntii. Under the Roman government it belonged to the province of Maxima Casariensis; subsequently it was included in the kingdom of Cumbria, which seems to have been the hereditary domain of the renowned King Arthur The inhabitants maintained their independence after the other parts of England had been conquered by the Saxons; but they were at length obliged to submit, and the co. was made a part of the Anglo-Saxon kingdom of Northumbria. At an early period the kings of Scotland acquired a kind of feudal authority over this co., which was the subject of frequent disputes between the English and Scottish sovereigns. It thus became exposed to the perpetual inroads of the Scots, and was the principal scene of border-warfare, which had a considerable effect on the character and manners of the inhabitants. The preservation of peace and the punishment of marauders were intrusted to a Lord-warden of the Northern-marches, -an officer of high responsibility and ample jurisdiction, and generally chosen from the great landed proprietors of this co.—The great Roman wall is the most remarkable antiquity of C. It was built by Adrian in 121, to exclude the Caledonians; and extended across the whole island from sea to sea. Its length was 100 m.; its breadth was 8 ft., and its height 12 ft. It had 25 strong castles planted, at intervals, in its course: the foundations of many of these, and of the wall itself, can still be traced.
CUMBERLAND, a township of Upper Canada, in the Ottawa district, to the SE of the Ottawa river. Pop. in 1842, 713.

CUMBERLAND, a river of the United States, which takes its rise in the Cumberland mountains, in the SE border of the state of Kentucky, runs in a WNW and SW direction, makes a considerable sweep through the northerly part of the state of Tennessee, passing Nashville near the extreme part of its southern bend, thence it re-enters Kentucky, and discharges itself into the Ohio, 111 m. above the mouth of the Tennessee, and 59 m. above the confluence of the Ohio with the Mississippi. The entire length of this river, the second in importance of the affluents of the Ohio, is 600 m.; and it drains a superficial area of 17,500 sq. m. It is navigable for large vessels to Nashville, a distance of 203 m., and at some seasons 200 m. higher; and for boats it is at all periods navigable for half its length. The elevation of its sources is supposed to exceed 1,000 ft.—Also a maritime county the capital. The soil is fertile and well-cultivated. superficies of 524 sq. m., bounded on the SSW by Delaware bay, and drained by Stow and Cohansey creeks, and Maurice and Tuckahoe rivers. It contains iron-ore and beds of marl. A salt marsh, from half-a-mile to 2 m., extends along Delaware bay. The soil is in some parts fertile. Pop. in 1830, 14,093; in 1840, 14,374. Its capital is Bridgetown.—Also a central county of the state of Pennsylvania, comprising an area of 544 sq. m., drained by Conedogwinit and Yellow Breeches creek, tributaries of the Susquehanna. The surface is hilly, and the soil extremely fertile. Iron-ore is found in some of its districts. Pop. in 1830, 29,228; in 1840, 30,953. Its capital is Carlisle.—Also a central county of the state of Virginia, containing a surface, generally hilly, of 320 sq. m., bounded on the SE by the Appomatox, and intersected by Willis rivers. It possesses considerable fertility. Pop. in 1830, 11,689; in 1840, 10,399. Its capital, Cumberland C. H., is situated on a high ridge between the two above-mentioned rivers, 52 m. SW of Richmond. Pop. in 1840, 90.-Also a county in the S part of the state of New Carolina, possessing an area of 1,300 sq. m., drained by Cape Fear river and its branches. Its surface is undulating, and its soil fertile. Pop. in 1830, 14,824; in 1840, 15,284. Its capital is Fayetteville.—Also a county in the S of the state of Kentucky, comprising an area of 270 sq. m., intersected by Cumberland river. It is hilly, but generally fertile. Pop. in 1840, 6,090. Its capital is Burkesville.—Also a township of Cumberland co., in the state of Maine, 42 m. SSW of Augusta, pleasantly and advantageously situated on Casco bay. Pop. in 1840, 1,616.—Also a village of Alleghany co., in the state of Maryland, 166 m. WNW of Annapolis, on the N bank of Potomac river, at the mouth of Will's creek, and intersected by the Chesapeake and Ohio canal, and the Baltimore and Ohio railroad. The Cumberland or National road, which runs W to the Ohio, commences at this village. Stone coal abounds in its vicinity.-Also a township of Adam's co., in the state of Pennsylvania, bounded on the E and W by respectively Rock and Marsh creeks, and intersected by their tributaries. Pop. in 1840, 1,217. Geltysburg lies in the E part of the township.—Also a village of Guernsey co., in the state of Ohio, 93 m. E of Columbus, on the Buffalo Fork of Will's creek.-Also a village of Marion co., in the state of Indiana, 10 m. E of Indianapolis, on the National road.—Also a township of Green co., in the state of Pennsylvania, 11 m. E of Waynesburg. It has a level surface, and is drained by Muddy creek, a branch of Monongahela river. Its soil is loamy. Pop. in 1840, 1,958. It contains the village of Lisburn.—Also a township of Providence co., in the state of Rhode island, 8 m. N of Providence. It is to some extent hilly, but is in some parts fertile and well-cultivated. Abbott's, Mill, and Peter's rivers, by which it is intersected, afford good water-power. Boat-building and the manufacture of cotton and woollen fabrics form the chief objects of local industry. Pop. 5,225. It contains a village named Cumberland-hill, on the NE side of Blackstone river, and 16 m. NW of Providence.

CUMBERLAND, an extensive peninsular district of British North America, extending from 64° 40' to 67° 30' N lat., between Baffin's bay on the ENE, Davis' straits on the E, and Northumberland inlet on the WSW.

CUMBERLAND, a county in New South Wales; bounded on the N by Northumberland; on the E by the ocean; on the S by Camden co.; and on the W by Cook co. Its coast-line commences at Broken bay, the estuary of the Hawkesbury, 16 m. N of Sydney, and stretches 56 m. S. It is about 40 m. in breadth, and has its W and N boundaries formed by the Hawkesbury; while the Cow-Pasture river skirts it on the SE. It contains the towns of Sydney, Paramatta, Windsor, and Liverpool; and the harbours of Broken bay, Port-Jackson, Botany bay, and Port-Hacking. The land bordering on the coast is of a light sandy nature, thinly sprinkled with bushes; from 10 to 15 m. interiorly it consists of a clayey ironstone soil; beyond this commences a fine timbered country. Along the banks of the Hawkesbury are alluvial lands of great fertility. Pop. in 1841, 58,108.

CUMBERLAND, a county of Nova Scotia, bounded on the N by Bay Verte and Northumberland strait; on the E by the co. of Picton; on the S by that of Colchester; and on the W by Chignecto bay, Cumberland basin, and New Brunswick. Pop. in 1837, 5,356. It is generally hilly, and in the central part rises into the Cobequid mountain. It is well- CUM watered, and contains some of the richest land in the prov.
CUMBERLAND BASIN, the E arm of Chignecto bay, in the bay of Fundy, New Brunswick, navigable to within 13 m. of Verte bay, in the gulf of St. Lawrence. The rise of tide here is 60 ft.

CUMBERLAND BAY, a bay on the coast of the island of Juan Fernandez, divided by its W head from English bay, in S lat. 33° 28', W long. 78° 45'. -Also a bay on the W coast of the island of St. Vincent, in N lat. 13° 14', W long. 61° 17'.
CUMBERLAND FORD, a village of Knox co., in the state of Kentucky, 135 m. SE of Frankfort, at the crossing of the great National road over Cumberland river.
CUMBERLAND FORT, or FORT BEAU SEJOUR, a fort on the N side of the Missiquash river, in New Brunswick, in N lat. 45° 49', W long. 64° 81'.
CUMBERLAND GAP, a village of Claiborne co., in the state of Tennessee, 17 m. S of Cumberland Ford, and 233 m. NE of Nashville, situated at a pass of the Cumberland mountains.
CUMBERLAND HARBOUR, a harbour on the N shore of the gulf of St. Lawrence, between Dukes island on the W, and Cumberland island on the E.

CUMBERLAND HARBOUR, or GUANTANAMO, an extensive and good harbour, composed of a series of deep lagunes, on the S coast of Cuba, 24 leagues W of Punta-de-Maysi. Its E head is in N lat. 19° 53' 28", W long. 75° 15' 45". It is sheltered from all winds, and abounds with fish. The country for a considerable distance inland is swampy, and full of salt marshes. Admiral Vernon gave it the name under which it is here described; by the early English adventurers it was called Walthenham harbour.

CUMBERLAND HOUSE, a station of the Hudson's Bay company, in N lat. 53° 56′ 40″, W long. 102° 21', at the upper extremity of a narrow island which separates Pine-Island lake from the Saskatchawan river, 690 m. from York factory. It consists of two log-houses surrounded by lofty stockades, and flanked with wooden bastions. The surrounding land is low, but the soil is good, and produces corn and vegetables. This factory was founded by Hearne, after his return from the Coppermine river, and has ever since been a post of considerable importance. The district, of which it is the centre of trading and hunting operations, extends about 150 m. from E to W along the Saskatchawan, and about as far from N to S, and comprehends about 20,000 sq. m. The Indians of this territory belong to the Cree nation. The fur-bearing animals of the district are black, silver, cross, red, and blue foxes; black and red bears, the lynx, marten, and beaver.
CUMBERLAND IRON WORKS, a village of Stewart co., in the state of Tennessee, on the S bank of Cumberland river, 6 m. E of Dover, and 69 m. NW of Nashville.

CUMBERLAND ISLAND, an island of Camden co., in the state of Georgia, extending along the coast, 15 m. in length, and from 1 to 5 in breadth. Its S end is in N lat. 30° 43′ 15", and W long. 81° 35' 30".—Also an island of the South Pacific, in the Low archipelago, in S. lat. 19° 20', and W long. 141° 20'. It was discovered by Wallis in 1767.

CUMBERLAND ISLES, a cluster of islands on the NE coast of Australia, between the parallels of 20° and 21° 6' S. They are from 600 to 800 ft. high; and all abundantly wooded, particularly with pines. They are of primitive formation.

CUMBERLAND MOUNTAINS, an extensive mountain-range of the United States, forming the western branch of the Alleghany chain, and extending from the SW corner of the state of Pennsylvania, to the banks of the Tennessee in Northern Alabama, a total distance of nearly 500 m. To the N of the 37th parallel this range is known by the name of the Laurel mountains. It runs along the confines of the states of Virginia and Kentucky, and covers with its ramifications the central part of the latter state forming the line of division between the beds of the Cumberland and Tennessee rivers. It is chiefly composed of enormous masses of craggy rocks. It contains numerous springs impregnated with alum, and abounds with limestone.
CUMBERLAND VALLEY, a township of Bedford co., in the state of Pennsylvania, between Evit's and Will's mountains, and traversed by Evit's creek. Pop. in 1840, 904.
CUMBERNAULD, a parish of Dumbartonshire, locally in Lanarkshire, comprising an area of 17,260 acres. Pop. 4,105. The village is 9 m. W of Falkirk, and 13 m. E of Glasgow. Pop. 2,227.
CUMBERSDALE, a township in the p. of St. Mary, Cumberland, 3 m. from Carlisle. Area 2,000 acres. Pop. 620.
CUMBERWORTH, a parish in Lincolnshire, 3\frac{1}{2} m. SE by E of Alford. Area 950 acres. Pop. 183.
CUMBERWORTH (HALF), a township in the p. of Kirk-Burton, W. R. of Yorkshire. Area 250 acres. Pop. 1,298.
CUMBERWORTH, a chapelry in the p. of Silkstone, W. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. SE of Huddersfield. Area, with part of the township of Skelmanthorpe, 930 acres. Pop. 1,867.

CUMBRAYS (THE), two islets in the frith of Clyde, between the island of Bute and the coast of Ayrshire, and belonging to the county of Bute. The Greater C., which is 4 m. E of the SE part of Bute and 2 m. W of Largs in Ayrshire, is 31 m. in length from NE to SW, and about 2 m. in breadth, comprising a superficial area of 5,100 sq. acres. A range of hills, called the Sheughends or Shoughends, runs across the island from N to S, and rises near the centre to the height of about 450 ft. Pop., comprising that of MILLPORT (which see), 1,413. The Little C. to the S of the Great C. is separated from it by a channel about 3 m. in breadth. It is about 1 m. in length, a 1 m. in breadth, and is 3 m. distant from the coast of Ayrshire. Pop. 8. On the W side, in N lat. 55° 43' and W long. 4° 58', is a lighthouse, which shows a fixed light to the distance in clear weather of 15 m. The two islands are a link in the geological chain which connects Bute with the main land.
CUMBRE (EL). See CANARY (GRAND).
CUMBRE, a town of Spain, in Estremadura, in the prov. and 20 m. ESE of Caceres, partido and 3 m. SW of Trujillo. Pop. 1,202. It has a parishchurch and a custom-house.

CUMBRESALTAS, or CUMBRES-MAYORES, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 60 m. NE of Huelva, partido of Aracena. Pop. 2,056. It has a parish-church, a custom-house, a convent, &c., and possesses some trade. Cattle, sheep, goats, pigs, &c., are extensively reared in the surrounding district.
CUMBRES-SAN-BARTOLOME', a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 57 m. NE of Huelva, partido of Aracena.

CUMBRES-DE-EUMEDIO, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 58 m. NE of Huelva, partido of Aracena. It derives its name from its situation between the towns of Cumbres-Mayores and Cumbres-San-Bartolomé.
CUMBRES ESPANOLAS, or SPANISH PEAKS, two lofty summits in Texas, on the dividing ridge between the waters of the Del-Norte and the Arkansas, in N lat. 37° 30'.

CUMBRIAN MOUNTAINS, a group spreading | CUM over part of Cumberland, Westmoreland, and N Lancashire, and united by the Shap fells to the Pennine mountains running into Yorkshire. The group covers an estimated area of 700 sq. m., and may be regarded as one immense mass of rock intersected by narrow and deep valleys. The line of its axis passes through Helvellyn, alt. 3,055 ft., Red Pike, alt., 2,893 ft. and along Shap fells.
CUMDEVOCK, a township in the p. of Dalston, Cumberland, 6 m. SSW of Carlisle. Pop. 361.
CUMIANA, a town of Piedmont, cap. of a mandamento, in the prov. and 10 m. N of Pinerolo, near the r. bank of the Cisola. Pop. 4,500.
CUMIERES, a commune of France, in the dep. and on the r. bank of the Marne, cant. of Ay, 15 m. SSW of Reims. Pop. 1,087. It affords good wine.

CUMILLAS, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 25 m. W of Santander, partido of San-Vicente-dela-Barguera, on a bay of the Atlantic, which forms the Concha-de-Cumillas. Pop. 707. It has a parishchurch, an hospital, and a custom-house; and has a considerable trade in cattle, and in cloth of local manufacture.
CUMINESTOWN, a village in the p. of Monquhitter, Aberdeenshire. Pop. 477.
CUMLA, or COOMBLA, a town and fort of Hindostan, in the prov. of Canara, 25 m. SSE of Mangalore, on a peninsula. It contains about 150 houses, and is inhabited chiefly by Hindoos and Mahommedans.

CUMMERAGH, or Monavoulagh, a lofty and conspicuous range of hills and mountains in the co. of Waterford. It bisects the co. into nearly equal parts; and extends from the immediate vicinity of Dungarvan to the vale of the Suir, midway between Clonmel and Carrick-on-Suir. The range is rather a series than a chain, consisting of heights which are only partially connected; and it has a somewhat irregular or sinuous direction, yet bears, in a general view, from S to N. The sides of the mountains are extremely wild and precipitous. Several lakes, called cummeloughs and stillogues, on the summit of the mountains, are highly picturesque. Clay-slate forms the great body of the mountains, and is, in some places, of a beautiful purple colour. Over this are found a close-grained, light-grey sandstone, argillaceous red sandstone, and an exceedingly beautiful slaty conglomerate.

CUMMERTREES, a parish in the district of Annandale, Dumfries-shire, comprising an area of upwards of 8,000 Scotch acres, extending along the coast between the Nith and Sark. Pop. 1,277. It contains a small v. of the same name; and a stream, called Cummertrees Pow, discharges itself into Queensferry bay. It is intersected by the Glasgow, Dumfries, and Carlisle railway, which has a station here 12 m. from Dumfries, and an equal distance from Gretna.
CUMMING, a village of Forsyth co., in the state of Georgia, U. S., 109 m. NW of Milledgeville, and 9 m. W of Chattahoochee river.
CUMMINGS, a township of Lycoming co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U.S., 111 m. N of Harrisburg. Pop. in 1840, 569.
CUMMINGSTON, a village in the p. of Duffus, Morayshire. Pop. 155.

CUMMINGTON, a township of Hampshire co., in the state of Massachusetts, U.S., 111 m. W of Boston. It has an uneven surface, but produces good pasturage. It is watered by Westfield river, which affords good water-power. Pop. in 1840, 1,237. It contains a flourishing village of the same name situated on Westfield river, 116 m. W of Boston.

CUMMUM, a town of Hindostan, cap. of the district of the same name, in the prov. of Balaghaut, division and 80 m. NNE of Cuddapah, situated to the E of a small lake, on an affluent of the Goondlagama, in N lat. 15° 35', and E long. 79° 12'. It is defended by a fort that was taken by the English in 1785, but soon after regained by Tippoo. The district is generally mountainous, but contains no rivers of importance. It was ceded to the British in 1800.

CUMMUMAIT, or KUMMUMMETT, a town and fortress of Hindostan, cap. of the district of the same name, in the prov. and 108 m. E of Hydrabad, on an affluent of the Kistnah, in N lat. 17° 15', and E long. 80° 12'. It has some cotton manufactures. The district is watered by the Godavery, which forms its E boundary, and by some of the affluents of the Kistnah. It is fertile, but ill-cultivated. It contains several considerable villages.

CUMNOCK (New), a parish in the district of Kyle, forming the SE limb of Ayrshire. It has an outline of nearly 12 m. in length from E to W, and somewhat more than 8 m. in breadth; and contains an area of upwards of 100 sq. m., or about 30,000 acres. Its surface is dotted with hills, and, in its southern division, is warted with mountains. Its highest elevations are Black-craig, about a 1/2 m. from its E boundary, rising 1,600 ft. above the valley of the Nith; and Black-Larg-hill, on its S boundary, which rises 2,890 ft. above sea-level. The lowest ground is the valley of the Nith,—a river which, rising in the SW extremity of the p., intersects it from W to E, and flows here about 500 ft. above sea-level. It is here a shallow and sluggish stream, highly tinctured with moss, and only 15 ft. broad. Carboniferous limestone occurs in abundance, in beds 12 ft. thick; and alternate seams of smiths' coal and cannel coal appear in the E district, and are in considerable request. Plumbago, or black-lead, is found in the coal-formation, and has, for a considerable period, been wrought; it is, however, of very inferior quality to that of Borrowdale in Cumberland. There are in the p. 3 villages, Path-head, Afton-Bridge-end, and New C.; which had in 1841, a pop.,—the first, of 325; the second, of 261; the third, of 161. Pop. of the p. in 1801, 1,381; in 1841, 2,382.

CUMNOCK (OLD), a parish in the E section of the district of Kyle in Ayrshire. It is about 10 m. in extreme length, by about 2 m. in average breadth, and is intersected from E to W by the Lugar. Pop. in 1801, 1,991; in 1841, 2,836.—The village of C. is situated in a sheltered hollow, at the confluence of the Lugar and the Glisnock, 101 m. SW of Muirkirk, and 16 m. E of Ayr, on the main road from Glasgow to Dumfries. The Glasgow, Dumfries, and Carlisle railway is carried across the Lugar about \( \frac{1}{4} \) m. above the v. by a viaduct of 14 arches, and 192 ft. high. The pop. are employed in weaving, which, when trade is good, keeps 120 looms at work; handsewing, a common employment with both adult and young females; the manufacture of thrashing-mills, which are in high esteem throughout the W of Scotland; and more especially the manufacture of wooden snuff-boxes, which, throughout Scotland, have, for their inimitable beauty, rendered-among snuff-takers at least—the v. surpassingly celebrated. In the last of these sources of support, C. is competed with only by Laurencekirk and Montrose. "A few years ago," says a writer in the New Statistical Account of Scotland, "a solid foot of wood that cost only 3s., could be manufactured into boxes worth £100 sterling, and then the workmanship increased the original value of the wood nearly 700 times; but at present a solid foot of wood, will only yield, in finished boxes, about £9 sterling." The great falling-off is to be accounted for chiefly by the satiating of the passion for novelty, -snuff-takers being as curious in the recherché of their box, as antiquarians are in the high date and freshness of their discoveries; and, in CUN a degree, by the sharpness of competition from the quarter whence the idea of the 'C. snuff-box' was originally obtained.
CUMQUIBUS, a parish of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, district of Baependi.
CUMREW, a parish of Cumberland, 6 m. N of Kirk-Oswald. Area 2,760 acres. Pop. 183.

CUMRU, a township of Berks co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., watered by Tulpehocken and Schuylkill rivers and their tributaries. The surface is partly hilly, and the soil, consisting of calcareous loam and gravel, is generally fertile. Pop. in 1840. 2,939.
CUMSHOHAW'S SOUND, a bay on the E side of Queen Charlotte's island, in the archipelago of New Hanover, North Pacific, in N lat. 52° 10', W long. 131° 30′.
CUMWHINTON-AND-COATHILL, a township in the p. of Wetheral, Cumberland, 4 m. SE of Carlisle. Pop. 339.
CUMWHITTON, a parish of Cumberland, 72 m. ESE of Carlisle, E of the Eden, and near the Newcastle and Carlisle railway. Area 5,670 acres. Pop. 533.

CUNDALL-AND-LECKBY, a parish and township in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 44 m. NNE of Borough Bridge, on the W bank of the Swale, and near the Great North of England railway. Area 3,150 acres. Pop. 378. Pop. of township 230.
CUNDAPOOR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Canava, S division, on the S side of the month of a river which flows into the Indian ocean, in N lat. 13° 38', and 55 m. NNW of Mangalore.

CUNDINAMARCA, a dep. of New Grenada, comprising the provs. of Antioquia, Mariquita, Neuva, and Bogota. It embraces the valleys of the Lower Cauca, and the Upper Magdalena, and the table-land of Bogota. Pop. 370,000.

CUNDWA, or Chundwa, a pergunnah and town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Candeish, division of Nemaur, 45 m. NNE of Burhanpur, in a plain, near the l. bank of an affluent of the Sukta. It is surrounded by a good mud wall, 15 ft. in height, and contained in 1820 houses to the number of 800. The pergunnah comprises an area of 580 sq. m., and contained at the above date 139 inhabited villages.
CUNE'GES, a village of France, in the dep. of the Dordogne, 8 m. SW of Bergerac. Pop. 462.

CUNENE, a river of Benguela, which, after the junction of the Cubango and several other streams to the E of Caconda, makes an extensive circuit along the SE and S frontiers of the kingdom, receiving in its course the Dumbo, Cuculo, and Dungui, and discharges itself under the name of Nourses river into the Atlantic, in S lat. 17° 30'.
CUNEO. See CONI.
CUNFIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Aube, cant. of Essoyes, near the source of the stream—an affluent of the Ource—of the same name. Pop. 1,119.

CUNHA, or FALCAO, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of São Paulo and comarca of Taubate, 125 m. ENE of São Paulo, and about the same distance W of Rio-de-Janeiro, near the Jacuhi, in S lat. 23° 3'. Pop. 3,000. Situated on the Serra Falcao, and within 20 m. of the coast, this town is noted for its salubrity; the temperature is lower than that of any other locality within the torrid region of Brazil. The environs are extremely fertile, and produce grain and varieties of tropical fruits in great abundance. The rearing of cattle, pigs, and fowls forms an important branch of local industry.
CUNHAU, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Rio- Grande-do-Norte, flowing into the sea 5 m. S of Ponta-da-Pipa.

CUNHINGA, or KUNJINGA, a river of Benguela, in the NE part of the kingdom, which flows NW through a district of the same name, and after a course of 225 m. falls into the Coanza, on the l. side, in W long. 17°.
